description: Matched primary Relation ground truth using neutral KO slots.
notes: The holdout material and annotations were authored only after the development method was frozen at commit 18e687d5cd7909531918b51e2d6bef38cb64a053.
All lectures, Knowledge Objects, candidate pairs, and pair-specific wording are new relative to Relation development.
Pair IDs are opaque and positive and hard-negative pairs are deliberately interleaved.
NO_RELATION is a benchmark-only label and must not be written to the product graph.
Neither baseline nor Prompt 002 had access to this file during development refinement.
Do not change labels, directions, evidence, scoring categories, or acceptable alternatives after either holdout prompt is run.
This artifact is deterministically derived; it is never model-facing.

evidence_spans.span: The likelihood is defined from this statistical model by treating the observations as fixed and comparing possible parameter values.

pairs.rationale: Likelihood is defined relative to the probability distributions supplied by a statistical model.
The bracket belongs to bisection, while the Newton update is presented separately; the lecture establishes no direct v0.1 Relation between them.

evidence_spans.span: Its path cost is the sum of their edge weights:

pairs.rationale: The displayed sum is the symbolic expression for path cost.

evidence_spans.span: An initial value problem combines an ordinary differential equation with an initial condition such as \(y(t_0)=y_0\).

pairs.rationale: An ordinary differential equation is one of the defining components of an initial value problem.
The formulas occur in the same estimation pipeline, but neither formula directly formalizes, requires, or is applied in the other formula under v0.1.

evidence_spans.span: The single-source shortest-path problem asks for a minimum-cost path from a chosen source vertex to every reachable vertex.
Dijkstra's algorithm is applied to this problem when all edge weights are nonnegative.

pairs.rationale: Dijkstra's algorithm is a method applied to the single-source shortest-path problem.

evidence_spans.span: The log-likelihood is obtained by taking the logarithm of the likelihood.
\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta; x_{1:n}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log p(x_i \mid \theta).

pairs.rationale: The displayed equation explicitly expresses the log-likelihood.

evidence_spans.span: Heun's method extends the forward Euler method with a predictor-corrector step.

pairs.rationale: Heun's method preserves the Euler predictor and enriches it with a correction.
Both objects are algorithms, but the holdout material supplies no educational Relation between root bracketing and shortest-path search.

evidence_spans.span: Maximum likelihood estimation uses the log-likelihood as its objective and selects a parameter value that maximizes it:

pairs.rationale: The log-likelihood is used as the objective in maximum likelihood estimation.
The step size controls a numerical discretization, but the lecture does not directly relate it to the mathematical definition of an initial value problem.

evidence_spans.span: The forward Euler method is applied to an initial value problem by advancing from the current approximation along the current vector-field slope.
y_{n+1} = y_n + hF(t_n,y_n).

pairs.rationale: The displayed update is the formal step performed by the forward Euler method.

evidence_spans.span: Newton's method and the bisection method contrast in the information they use: Newton's method requires derivative values and can converge rapidly near a root, whereas bisection uses only function signs and preserves a bracket at every iteration.

pairs.rationale: The lecture explicitly contrasts the information and convergence behavior of the two methods.

evidence_spans.span: Its path cost is the sum of their edge weights:
The single-source shortest-path problem asks for a minimum-cost path from a chosen source vertex to every reachable vertex.

pairs.rationale: Understanding the shortest-path objective requires the definition of path cost.
Both objects assign numerical values to alternatives in their own domains, but the holdout texts establish no educational Relation between likelihood and path cost.

evidence_spans.span: The likelihood is defined from this statistical model by treating the observations as fixed and comparing possible parameter values.
L(\theta; x_{1:n}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i \mid \theta).

pairs.rationale: The product formula explicitly expresses the likelihood for independent observations.

evidence_spans.span: The forward Euler method is applied to an initial value problem by advancing from the current approximation along the current vector-field slope.

pairs.rationale: Forward Euler is explicitly presented as a numerical method applied to an initial value problem.

evidence_spans.span: A* search extends Dijkstra's algorithm by using a heuristic function \(h(v)\) to guide which vertex is explored next.

pairs.rationale: A* retains Dijkstra's path-cost ordering as a base and enriches it with a heuristic.

evidence_spans.span: Newton's method uses the derivative to replace the function locally by its tangent line.
x_{k+1} = x_k - \frac{f(x_k)}{f'(x_k)}.

pairs.rationale: The displayed iteration is the update rule defining Newton's method.

evidence_spans.span: When this equation has no closed-form solution, Newton's method can be applied within maximum likelihood estimation to compute a candidate numerically.

pairs.rationale: The statistics lecture explicitly applies Newton's method within maximum likelihood estimation.

evidence_spans.span: Relaxation is formalized by the update
d(v) \leftarrow \min\{d(v),\, d(u)+w(u,v)\}.

pairs.rationale: The minimum-distance assignment is the formal update performed by edge relaxation.

evidence_spans.span: The bisection method begins with a sign-changing bracket \([a,b]\) satisfying \(f(a)f(b)<0\).

pairs.rationale: The sign-changing bracket is the required starting condition for bisection in the lecture.

evidence_spans.span: The log-likelihood is obtained by taking the logarithm of the likelihood.

pairs.rationale: The log-likelihood is defined by transforming the likelihood.

evidence_spans.span: Dijkstra's algorithm stores tentative distance estimates in a priority queue.
After removing a vertex \(u\) with the smallest tentative distance, it applies edge relaxation to each outgoing edge.

pairs.rationale: Dijkstra's algorithm repeatedly applies edge relaxation to update tentative distances.
The lecture contrasts ordinary Newton's method with bisection but does not directly compare or type a Relation between damped Newton and bisection.

evidence_spans.span: The forward Euler method is applied to an initial value problem by advancing from the current approximation along the current vector-field slope.

pairs.rationale: Forward Euler uses the current vector-field slope to construct each step.

evidence_spans.span: Maximum likelihood estimation uses the log-likelihood as its objective and selects a parameter value that maximizes it:
\widehat{\theta}_{\mathrm{MLE}} = \operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\theta}\, \ell(\theta).

pairs.rationale: The argmax formula is the formal optimization statement of maximum likelihood estimation.
Both are formulas, but the holdout texts establish no learning Relation between statistical estimation and Euler time stepping.

evidence_spans.span: The damped Newton method extends Newton's method by scaling the Newton correction with a factor between zero and one.

pairs.rationale: Damped Newton retains the Newton correction and adds a scaling control.

evidence_spans.span: A* search extends Dijkstra's algorithm by using a heuristic function \(h(v)\) to guide which vertex is explored next.

pairs.rationale: A* uses the heuristic function to guide its exploration order.

evidence_spans.span: Heun's method extends the forward Euler method with a predictor-corrector step.
The corrected update is
y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}\left(F(t_n,y_n)+F(t_n+h,\widetilde{y}_{n+1})\right).

pairs.rationale: The corrected formula gives the update rule for Heun's predictor-corrector method.

evidence_spans.span: The bisection method begins with a sign-changing bracket \([a,b]\) satisfying \(f(a)f(b)<0\).
It evaluates the midpoint
m = \frac{a+b}{2}

pairs.rationale: The midpoint equation formalizes the point evaluated at each bisection step.
The holdout materials provide no Relation between differentiating a log-likelihood and updating graph distances.
